schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul, about to rebuke Israel more vehemently, first removes every suspicion of hatred — for it is not likely the same person should desire their salvation, pray for it, and yet hate them; he calls his exceeding desire and prayer his 'heart's desire'; Paul's heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel that they might be saved — the apostle's deep love for his own people (Romans 9:1-3) even amid their unbelief and his coming rebuke (the rebuke flowing from love, Proverbs 27:6, the severity from tenderness), the example of the gospel heart longing and praying for the salvation of the lost.

themes: my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel
that they might be saved
the apostle's love for his people
the rebuke flowing from love

schools.summary: Gill: Israel had a zeal for God — for his being and unity (against the idolatry of the Gentiles), for the word of God, the law (moral and ceremonial), and the worship of God, sparing no pains to make proselytes; Paul bearing them record that they have a zeal of God but not according to knowledge — the sincere but misdirected zeal (earnest for God yet ignorant of the true way of righteousness, v.3, opposing the gospel it should have embraced, Acts 26:9), the peril of zeal without knowledge (the sincere may be sincerely wrong, zeal no proof of truth), Paul's fair acknowledgment ('I bear them record'), the warning to the religious.

themes: they have a zeal of God
but not according to knowledge
sincere but misdirected zeal
the peril of zeal without knowledge

schools.summary: Gill: they were ignorant of the righteousness of God revealed in the gospel (the righteousness of Christ) and of the spirituality of the law (which requires conformity of heart, not merely outward obedience); being ignorant of God's righteousness, going about to establish their own (by the works of the law), they did not submit to the righteousness of God — the threefold root of legalism (ignorance of God's righteousness, the self-righteous effort to establish their own, the refusal to submit), the pride that would not stoop to receive the free righteousness of Christ by faith (the contrast of God's righteousness and their own).

themes: ignorant of God's righteousness
going about to establish their own righteousness
have not submitted... unto the righteousness of God
the anatomy of legalism

schools.summary: Gill: 'the law' here the moral law, of which Christ is the end — the end in respect of its perfect fulfilment (he fulfilled it for righteousness) and the end of it as a way of justification (terminating its use for that purpose); Christ the end (telos) of the law for righteousness to every believer — the rich phrase embracing the goal/aim the law pointed to (its types and demands leading to him), the termination of the law as a way of justification, and the fulfilment (Christ perfectly keeping it); the believer having in Christ the righteousness the law demanded but could not give, received by faith ('to every one that believeth'), against Israel's self-righteousness (v.3).

debates.question: What does 'Christ is the end of the law' mean?

sides.position: Christ the goal/aim the law pointed to, the termination of the law as a way of justification, and its fulfilment (the rich phrase embracing all)

themes: Christ is the end of the law
for righteousness to every one that believeth
the goal and termination of the law
the believer's righteousness by faith

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle wisely gives the description in the words of Moses (esteemed among the Jews) — the righteousness the law requires is that the man who does those things shall live by them (Leviticus 18:5); the principle of legal righteousness — do and live (the law promising life only to perfect, continual obedience to all of it, Galatians 3:10); the impossibility for sinners (none perfectly doing, the law leaving all under condemnation, the way of life closed by works), set against the righteousness of faith (vv.6-9, the doing vs the believing); the law's unattainable demand driving the sinner to Christ (the schoolmaster, Galatians 3:24).

themes: the righteousness which is of the law
the man which doeth those things shall live by them
legal righteousness by perfect doing
which none attains

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul does not claim Moses formally described justification by faith, but expresses the language of faith mainly in words Moses used (Deuteronomy 30:12-14) which aptly express its meaning — the thing not remote and difficult but near; the righteousness of faith speaking differently from the law (v.5) — 'Say not... Who shall ascend into heaven? (to bring Christ down)' as if Christ had not come, for the work is already done (Christ has come, the incarnation accomplished, no need to bring him down); faith resting on the finished work of Christ, not on impossible human achievement (the contrast with the law's 'do and live'), the way of faith accessible, not remote.

themes: the righteousness which is of faith speaketh
Say not... Who shall ascend into heaven?
to bring Christ down from above
faith rests on the finished work

schools.summary: Barnes: these words also part of the address of Moses (Deuteronomy 30:13), not literally quoted (the Hebrew speaks of 'beyond the sea') but the sense expressed in Moses's words, Paul expressing the language of faith (the words referring to that which is remote and difficult); faith not saying 'Who shall descend into the deep? (to bring up Christ from the dead)' as if Christ were not risen, for Christ is already risen (the resurrection accomplished, no need to descend); faith resting on the completed work — the incarnation (v.6) and resurrection (v.7) accomplished — needing only to believe (not to ascend, descend, or achieve, but to receive), against the law's impossible demand (v.5).

themes: Who shall descend into the deep?
to bring up Christ again from the dead
Christ already risen
faith rests on the completed work

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the word is nigh thee' still the language of Moses (Deuteronomy 30:14), the meaning that the doctrine is not difficult to be understood and embraced (what is nigh us may be easily obtained); the word near — in the mouth (to be confessed) and in the heart (to be believed) — that is, the word of faith which the apostles preach (the gospel, the doctrine of justification by faith); the accessibility of the gospel (the word of faith brought close, ready to be received, not remote like the law's impossible demand of ascending, descending, doing all), the word of faith preached the means of faith (v.17).

themes: the word is nigh thee, even in thy mouth, and in thy heart
the word of faith, which we preach
the gospel accessible, not remote
the word brought near

schools.summary: Gill: the confession a good, sincere, hearty confession before the church and the world that Christ is one's Lord and Saviour, arising from a true faith in the heart; if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus and believe in thine heart that God raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved — the joining of the inward faith (believing the resurrection, the proof of Christ's deity and the acceptance of his work, the ground of justification, Romans 4:25) and the outward confession (owning Jesus as Lord, his deity, authority, sovereignty); the order — faith the root in the heart, confession the fruit with the mouth.

themes: confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus
believe in thine heart that God hath raised him
the inward faith and outward confession
the way of salvation

schools.summary: Gill: true faith does not lie in the bare assent of the mind, nor in the brain, nor in the tongue, but in the heart — a believing with all the heart, in which all the powers of the soul (understanding, will, affections) are concerned; with the heart man believeth unto righteousness (the inward faith laying hold of the righteousness of Christ imputed, the root of justification) and with the mouth confession is made unto salvation (the outward confession owning Christ, the open avowal of the inward faith) — faith and confession distinguished (the inward and the outward) yet joined (the true faith confesses, the confession springs from faith), the root and the fruit.

themes: with the heart man believeth unto righteousness
with the mouth confession is made unto salvation
heart-faith the root, mouth-confession the avowal
faith and confession joined

schools.summary: Gill: the passage (Isaiah 28:16, cited before in Romans 9:33) produced to prove the connection between faith and righteousness, confession and salvation — in the prophet 'he that believeth,' but here a universal 'whosoever' added; whosoever believeth on Christ shall not be ashamed — the promise that no believer shall be put to shame (his faith never disappointed, his hope never confounded, resting on Christ who never fails), the universal 'whosoever' extending the promise to all who believe (Jew and Gentile alike, v.12), the contrast with the self-righteous (who shall be ashamed, their own righteousness failing, v.3).

themes: whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed
the universal whosoever
the believer never disappointed
the promise to all believers

schools.summary: Gill: no distinction of nations, no superiority by carnal descent or fleshly privileges, no preeminence by the former dispensation's ordinances (all now abolished), nor difference in their state Godward (all under sin and needing Christ's righteousness); there is no difference between Jew and Greek, for the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call upon him — the breaking down of the Jew/Gentile distinction in the gospel (the middle wall broken, Ephesians 2:14, the one way for all), the universal lordship of Christ (over all nations) and his unsearchable riches (Ephesians 3:8, abounding in mercy and grace to all who call).

themes: no difference between the Jew and the Greek
the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call
the universal lordship of Christ
the riches of Christ to all

schools.summary: Gill and Barnes: this testimony from Joel 2:32, applied to the times of the gospel (as Peter in Acts 2:21); whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved — the universal promise of salvation to all who call (without distinction, v.12, invoking the Lord in faith and prayer, seeking his salvation); the striking application — the 'Lord' of Joel (Yahweh, the LORD) here applied to Christ (for it is on Christ the Lord that men call to be saved, vv.9, 12) — a strong testimony to his deity (to call on the Lord for salvation is to call on God, the divine Saviour).

debates.question: Whose name is 'the name of the Lord' that saves?

sides.position: The 'Lord' of Joel (Yahweh) is applied to Christ — men are saved by calling on Christ as Lord, a testimony to his deity

themes: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
the universal call
the name of the Lord (Yahweh) applied to Christ
a testimony to his deity

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul here takes from Israel all excuse — having said they had a zeal but not according to knowledge and were ignorant, he shows that for this ignorance itself they were punishable, framing the passage out of questions and answers; the chain traced back — they cannot call on one they have not believed, nor believe in one they have not heard, nor hear without a preacher (the divinely-ordained sequence by which faith comes: calling resting on believing, believing on hearing, hearing on preaching, v.17), the necessity of the preached gospel and the sent preacher (v.15), the missionary import (the gospel carried to those who have not heard).

themes: how shall they call on him in whom they have not believed?
how shall they hear without a preacher?
the chain of faith
the necessity of the preached gospel

schools.summary: Gill: no proper, lawful preaching without a mission — either extraordinary (the apostles', immediately by Christ, with miraculous gifts) or ordinary (of pastors and teachers, sent by Christ through the church); how shall they preach unless they be sent? — the necessity of the divine sending of preachers (the lawful commission, the herald sent by the King); and (citing Isaiah 52:7) the beauty of the feet of those who preach the gospel of peace and bring glad tidings of good things — the welcome beauty of the gospel messengers (the feet welcome and lovely for the good news they bring, the gospel of peace/reconciliation, the dignity of the ministry).

themes: how shall they preach, except they be sent?
how beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace
the necessity of the divine sending
the welcome beauty of the messengers

schools.summary: Gill: though ministers may be regularly sent and rightly preach the gospel in its purity, there is no success without the power of God attending it — men may hear and yet not obey (not cordially embrace the doctrines nor submit to the ordinances); not all have obeyed the gospel, and Isaiah saith (53:1) 'Lord, who hath believed our report?' — the sad fact of unbelief despite the preaching (the gospel preached yet not received by all, the prophet's lament over the few who believe), the necessity of God's power for the hearing to become believing (the obedience of faith, Romans 1:5), Isaiah's lament fulfilled in the Jews' unbelief.

themes: they have not all obeyed the gospel
Lord, who hath believed our report?
unbelief despite the preaching
God's power needed for faith

schools.summary: Gill: 'hearing' used as in the preceding verse, designing the report of the gospel, the preaching of the word, which is the means God uses to convey faith into the hearts of his people (preachers the instruments by whom others believe); faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God — the ordained means of faith (the preaching of the gospel heard, the Spirit working faith through the heard word, 1 Corinthians 1:21, 'by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe'), the word of God the instrument and the warrant of the preaching (the message preached God's own word, not man's), the word indispensable.

themes: faith cometh by hearing
hearing by the word of God
the ordained means of faith
the preached word the instrument

schools.summary: Barnes: Paul replies to the objection (that men were condemned for not believing what they had not heard) — not denying the principle but affirming they had heard, the gospel widely proclaimed; have they not heard? Yes verily — citing Psalm 19:4 (originally of the heavens' witness) and applying it to the gospel, 'their sound went into all the earth' (the wide proclamation, the message everywhere in the apostolic age, the gospel's reach to the known world); the gospel leaving the unbelieving without excuse (they have heard and not believed, the responsibility, the guilt of unbelief inexcusable).

themes: Have they not heard? Yes verily
their sound went into all the earth
the wide proclamation of the gospel
the unbelieving without excuse

schools.summary: Gill: Israel did know — they knew God, his will, the right worship, and even the gospel (not spiritually but nationally and speculatively, against the light and conviction); Paul citing Moses (Deuteronomy 32:21) — God would provoke Israel to jealousy by 'them that are no people' (the Gentiles) and anger them by a foolish nation; Israel's knowledge making them inexcusable in unbelief, and the call of the Gentiles foretold by Moses (the no-people made a people, Hosea 2:23) — the Gentiles' reception of the grace Israel refused provoking Israel to jealousy (the design of mercy, that Israel might be stirred to seek the grace they refused, Romans 11:11).

themes: Did not Israel know?
provoke you to jealousy by them that are no people
Israel inexcusable
the call of the Gentiles foretold

schools.summary: Gill: Isaiah was 'very bold' — he did not mince the matter or cover it with dark sayings, but with all plainness, courage, and intrepidity declared the calling of the Gentiles, though he ran the risk of his credit and even his life among the Jews; Paul citing Isaiah (65:1) — God was found of them that sought him not, made manifest to them that asked not after him; the sovereign, prevenient grace that found the Gentiles who did not seek God (the unsought mercy, God seeking the unseeking, 1 John 4:19, 'we love him because he first loved us'); the contrast with Israel (the unseeking Gentiles found, the privileged Israel refusing, v.21, the reversal).

themes: I was found of them that sought me not
made manifest unto them that asked not after me
the sovereign grace that seeks the unseeking
the boldness of Isaiah

schools.summary: Barnes: while the Gentiles would be obedient, the character of the Jews was that they were a disobedient and rebellious people; 'all day long' means continually, without intermission; Paul citing Isaiah (65:2) — all day long God stretched forth his hands to a disobedient and gainsaying people — the image of God's patient, persistent pleading (the outstretched hands of invitation, entreaty, and welcome, the long-suffering, Matthew 23:37, 'how often would I have gathered... and ye would not') met by Israel's continual disobedience and contradiction, closing the chapter on the tragedy of the privileged people's unbelief (while the unseeking Gentiles found mercy, v.20).

themes: all day long I have stretched forth my hands
unto a disobedient and gainsaying people
the patient pleading of God
the tragedy of Israel's unbelief
